title: Single-Collection Design & Multi-Value Indexes
description: The universal NoSQL principle — what is accessed together should be stored together — refined with compound indexes, multi-value indexes, and the full power of SQL analytics.
introduction:
There is a universal principle in data modeling that predates any specific database: what is accessed together should be stored together. In relational systems, normalization optimizes for storage efficiency and update consistency — and then you pay for it at read time with multi-table joins. In document systems, you have a choice. You can embed related data inside a single document for fast reads, or you can store related documents separately in the same collection and use indexes to collect them.

That choice is not a philosophy decision. It is a physics decision. The right answer depends on your storage engine, your access patterns, and the size of your documents.

Consider the tradeoffs. Embedding an advisor's entire client roster inside a single advisor document gives you one-read access to the full entity graph. But when a client's portfolio value changes — which happens constantly in financial services — you are rewriting the entire advisor document for a single field update. If that document is 2KB, the overhead is negligible. If it is 200KB, you are moving 200KB of data for a 8-byte number change. On MongoDB, WiredTiger writes 32KB leaf pages regardless of how much data changed within them. On Oracle, documents under roughly 8KB are stored inline with the row in the table's data blocks. Cross that threshold and the document migrates to LOB storage — a fundamentally different I/O path with different performance characteristics. These are the physics of your storage engine, and they determine whether embedding or separating is the right model.

Single-collection design refines the universal principle by taking these physics into account. Instead of embedding everything into one massive document, you store small, focused documents — each representing a single entity like an advisor profile, a client summary, or a holding — together in the same collection. Compound indexes on shared attributes group them, so a single indexed query retrieves the entire related set in one round trip. You get the locality benefit of embedding without the write amplification penalty. And because each document is small, you stay within your storage engine's sweet spot — inline storage on Oracle, efficient page utilization on MongoDB.

[svg:m3-single-table.svg]

The collection in this module — advisory_entities — stores advisors, clients, holdings, and transactions as separate documents with natural domain attributes. Advisor and client documents share an advisorId attribute, so a single indexed query retrieves an advisor with all their clients. Holding and transaction documents share an accountId, grouping them by account. Every holding carries a symbol attribute, enabling cross-account lookups. No artificial key encoding. No concatenated prefixes. Just natural attributes with compound indexes — the same pattern that works on any document database.

This is where Oracle transforms the pattern. On a pure document database, single-collection design gives you fast point reads and bounded range scans within an index prefix. That is all. Cross-group analytics require application-side aggregation. Joining entity types requires multiple queries stitched together in code. On Oracle, you keep the locality benefits but gain the full SQL engine: joins across entity types within the collection, GROUP BY and window functions for cross-group analytics, CTEs for multi-step computations, and JSON_TABLE for shredding arrays into relational rows. And you get CREATE MULTIVALUE INDEX — full B-tree indexes on every element inside a JSON array, queryable individually or combined. MongoDB's parallel arrays restriction prohibits compound indexes on two array fields. Oracle has no such limitation.

By the end of this module, you will have explored the entity collection, created compound indexes for multiple access patterns, built multi-value indexes on arrays and combined them in queries that other document databases physically cannot execute, and run SQL analytics that would require a separate analytical database on any other platform. The universal principle, refined by storage engine physics, amplified by SQL convergence.
checkpoint: One collection. Four entity types. Natural attributes with compound indexes. SQL joins, aggregation, and multi-value indexes — all on the same collection.

exercises.title: Explore the Entity Collection
exercises.description: See how multiple entity types co-exist in one collection, query by shared attributes, and filter by entity type.
exercises.explanation:
This is single-collection design in practice — the universal principle that what is accessed together should be stored together, applied at the collection level. Four entity types — advisors, clients, holdings, and transactions — live together in the advisory_entities collection. Each document uses **natural domain attributes** for grouping. Advisor and client documents share **advisorId** — querying `WHERE ae.data.advisorId.number() = 5` retrieves the advisor profile and all their clients in one scan. Holding and transaction documents share **accountId** for account-level grouping. Every holding and transaction carries a **symbol** for cross-account lookups.

[svg:m3-entity-collection.svg]

The storage engine physics matter here. Each document in this collection is small — typically 200 to 800 bytes. On Oracle, documents under roughly 8KB are stored inline with the row in the table's data blocks, which means a single block read pulls in multiple entity documents. Compare this to the embedding approach where an advisor document containing all nested clients and holdings could easily exceed 8KB, crossing the threshold into LOB storage — a fundamentally different I/O path with higher latency per access. On MongoDB's WiredTiger engine, the same physics apply at the page level: smaller documents mean more entities per 32KB leaf page, which means fewer page reads for range scans. The single-collection pattern keeps documents small and co-located.

There are no artificial key encodings, no concatenated prefixes like `ADVISOR#5` or `CLIENT#5#42`. The **entityType** field discriminates between advisors, clients, holdings, and transactions using plain domain values. You can count by type, filter within a group, or aggregate across the entire collection. On Oracle, the cost-based optimizer treats JSON path expressions like `ae.data.advisorId.number()` exactly like relational column predicates, choosing index scans or full scans based on data statistics. The dot-notation accessors — `.string()`, `.number()`, `.boolean()` — are not just syntactic sugar. They declare the expected data type, enabling the optimizer to use typed B-tree indexes and avoid runtime type coercion.

**Try this in the editor:**
- `SELECT DISTINCT ae.data.advisorId.number() AS advisor_id FROM advisory_entities ae WHERE ae.data.advisorId.number() IS NOT NULL ORDER BY 1` — see all advisor IDs in the collection
- `SELECT ae.data.entityType.string() AS type, json_serialize(ae.data PRETTY) AS doc FROM advisory_entities ae WHERE ae.data.advisorId.number() = 3 AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'client'` — explore a different advisor's clients
exercises.code:
-- See all entity types in one collection
SELECT ae.data.entityType.string() AS entity_type,
       COUNT(*) AS count
FROM advisory_entities ae
GROUP BY ae.data.entityType.string()
ORDER BY count DESC;

-- Query by shared attribute — get an advisor and all their clients
SELECT ae.data.entityType.string() AS type,
       json_serialize(ae.data PRETTY) AS document
FROM advisory_entities ae
WHERE ae.data.advisorId.number() = 5
ORDER BY ae.data.entityType.string();

-- Filter by entity type — get just the clients for an advisor
SELECT ae.data.firstName.string() || ' ' || ae.data.lastName.string() AS client_name,
       ae.data.riskProfile.string() AS risk_profile,
       ae.data.totalValue.number() AS total_value
FROM advisory_entities ae
WHERE ae.data.advisorId.number() = 5
  AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'client'
ORDER BY ae.data.totalValue.number() DESC;

steps.label: Count all entity types in the collection
steps.code:
SELECT ae.data.entityType.string() AS entity_type,
       COUNT(*) AS count
FROM advisory_entities ae
GROUP BY ae.data.entityType.string()
ORDER BY count DESC;

steps.label: Query by advisorId — get an advisor and all their clients
steps.code:
SELECT ae.data.entityType.string() AS type,
       json_serialize(ae.data PRETTY) AS document
FROM advisory_entities ae
WHERE ae.data.advisorId.number() = 5
ORDER BY ae.data.entityType.string();

steps.label: Filter by entity type — get just the clients for an advisor
steps.code:
SELECT ae.data.firstName.string() || ' ' || ae.data.lastName.string() AS client_name,
       ae.data.riskProfile.string() AS risk_profile,
       ae.data.totalValue.number() AS total_value
FROM advisory_entities ae
WHERE ae.data.advisorId.number() = 5
  AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'client'
ORDER BY ae.data.totalValue.number() DESC;

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM advisory_entities WHERE json_value(data, '$.entityType') IS NOT NULL
exercises.checkpoint: You just queried a single collection containing four entity types using natural domain attributes. One query on advisorId retrieves both the advisor and their clients — the universal principle in action.

exercises.title: Compound Indexes for Access Patterns
exercises.description: Create indexes on natural attributes to support advisor, account, and symbol access patterns.
exercises.explanation:
Every access pattern your application needs should resolve to an indexed lookup — a point read or a bounded range scan. Without indexes, querying `WHERE ae.data.advisorId.number() = 5` requires a full collection scan: every document read, every JSON payload parsed, every non-matching row discarded. With an index, it is a B-tree probe followed by a bounded range scan — logarithmic versus linear cost.

[svg:m3-compound-indexes.svg]

The index on **advisorId** supports the advisor-centric pattern: retrieve an advisor and all their clients. The index on **accountId** supports the account-centric pattern: retrieve all holdings and transactions for an account. The index on **symbol** enables a cross-cutting access pattern: find every holding or transaction for a given stock symbol across all accounts. The syntax is `CREATE INDEX idx_name ON collection ae (ae.data.path.type())` — the dot-notation accessor declares the JSON path and the expected type, and Oracle builds a standard B-tree on the extracted values. No special API. No index definition documents. Just DDL.

This is where Oracle's approach differs fundamentally from databases that require you to encode access patterns into key structures. On DynamoDB, every access pattern beyond the primary key requires a Global Secondary Index with its own throughput provisioning and eventual consistency lag. On MongoDB, you design compound indexes carefully because index intersection is unreliable in the query planner. On Oracle, you simply create an index on the attribute you want to query. The cost-based optimizer decides when and how to use each index, and can **combine multiple indexes in a single query plan using bitmap conversion**. A query filtering on both `advisorId` and `entityType` can use two separate indexes, convert both result sets to bitmaps, AND them together, and probe the table only for matching rows. There is no arbitrary limit on the number of indexes, no replication lag, and no storage duplication beyond the B-tree itself.

The tradeoff is write overhead. Each index adds a B-tree maintenance cost on INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE. For a read-heavy workload like advisor portfolio lookups, three indexes are trivially worth it. For a write-heavy event stream, you would be more selective. The right answer is always the read/write ratio of your actual access patterns.

**Try this in the editor:**
- `SELECT ae.data.symbol.string() AS symbol, COUNT(*) AS holdings FROM advisory_entities ae WHERE ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding' GROUP BY ae.data.symbol.string() ORDER BY 2 DESC` — see which symbols appear most frequently across all accounts

steps.label: Create index on advisorId for advisor + client lookups
Create index on accountId for holdings + transaction lookups
Create index on symbol for cross-account lookups
Use the symbol index to find all AAPL holdings
steps.code:
SELECT ae.data.accountId.number() AS account_id,
       ae.data.quantity.number() AS quantity,
       ae.data.marketValue.number() AS market_value
FROM advisory_entities ae
WHERE ae.data.symbol.string() = 'AAPL'
  AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding';

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM user_indexes WHERE index_name IN ('IDX_ADVISOR', 'IDX_ACCOUNT', 'IDX_SYMBOL')
exercises.checkpoint: You just created three indexes on natural domain attributes — advisorId, accountId, symbol. Each one enables a different access pattern. No artificial key encoding. No index limits. No replication lag.

exercises.title: Multi-Value Indexes — Index Into Arrays
exercises.description: Create multi-value indexes on tags and sectors arrays, build a compound multivalue index, and intersect indexes across multiple array fields.
exercises.explanation:
**CREATE MULTIVALUE INDEX** lets Oracle build a B-tree index on every element inside a JSON array. If a holding document has `"tags": ["sp500", "dividend", "growth"]`, three separate index entries are created — one per array element. When you query `JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.tags?(@ == "dividend")')`, the optimizer resolves it via a standard B-tree probe, not a full collection scan. This is critical for any domain with tags, categories, sectors, or multi-valued attributes.

[svg:m3-multivalue-index.svg]

The storage engine physics of multi-value indexes are straightforward. For an array with N elements, the index stores N entries per document instead of one. If your average tags array has 3 elements, the multi-value index is roughly 3x the size of a scalar index on the same collection. That is the write amplification tradeoff: each INSERT or UPDATE to the document must maintain all N index entries. For relatively static metadata like tags and sectors — which describe the nature of a holding, not its current state — this tradeoff is clearly worth it. If these tags changed on every market tick, you would think harder about the cost.

Embedding tags inside the holding document is the right call because it avoids a join on every read, and the multi-value index makes each array element individually queryable. If these tags changed frequently, you might store them as separate documents and join on demand — trading read overhead for write efficiency.

Both MongoDB and Oracle support compound indexes with one scalar field and one array field — MongoDB calls these **multikey indexes**, Oracle uses the **compound multivalue** syntax via `JSON_TABLE`. Both share the same restriction: you cannot create a single compound index on **two sibling array fields**, because the cross-product produces combinatorial explosion. The key difference is what happens when you need to query across two separate array indexes. Oracle's cost-based optimizer reliably **intersects separate multivalue indexes** using bitmap conversion — combining results from an index on tags with results from an index on sectors without scanning any non-matching document. MongoDB's query planner supports index intersection in theory, but in practice frequently falls back to a single index plus collection filter. At scale, that fallback means scanning every document matching one predicate to evaluate the second. Oracle's bitmap approach combines two index result sets and probes only the matching rows.

**Try this in the editor:**
- `SELECT ae.data.symbol.string(), json_serialize(ae.data.tags) FROM advisory_entities ae WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.tags?(@ == "growth")') AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding'` — find growth-tagged holdings and see their full tag sets
exercises.code:
-- Create a multi-value index on the tags array
CREATE MULTIVALUE INDEX idx_tags ON advisory_entities ae
    (ae.data.tags.string());

-- Create a multi-value index on the sectors array
CREATE MULTIVALUE INDEX idx_sectors ON advisory_entities ae
    (ae.data.sectors.string());

-- Compound multivalue index: scalar entityType + array tags in ONE index
CREATE MULTIVALUE INDEX idx_holding_tags ON advisory_entities ae
    (JSON_TABLE(ae.data, '$'
        COLUMNS (
            entity_type VARCHAR2(50) PATH '$.entityType'
                ERROR ON ERROR NULL ON EMPTY NULL ON MISMATCH,
            NESTED PATH '$.tags[*]' COLUMNS (tag VARCHAR2(50) PATH '$'
                ERROR ON ERROR NULL ON EMPTY NULL ON MISMATCH)
        )
    ));

-- Find all holdings tagged "dividend" (uses compound index: entityType + tags)
SELECT ae.data.symbol.string() AS symbol,
       ae.data.quantity.number() AS quantity,
       ae.data.marketValue.number() AS market_value,
       json_serialize(ae.data.tags) AS tags
FROM advisory_entities ae
WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.tags?(@ == "dividend")')
  AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding';

-- Intersect two multivalue indexes via bitmap conversion
-- (find S&P 500 holdings in the technology sector)
SELECT ae.data.symbol.string() AS symbol,
       ae.data.marketValue.number() AS market_value
FROM advisory_entities ae
WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.tags?(@ == "sp500")')
  AND JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.sectors?(@ == "technology")')
  AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding';

steps.label: Create a multi-value index on the tags array
steps.code:
CREATE MULTIVALUE INDEX idx_tags ON advisory_entities ae
    (ae.data.tags.string());

steps.label: Create a multi-value index on the sectors array
steps.code:
CREATE MULTIVALUE INDEX idx_sectors ON advisory_entities ae
    (ae.data.sectors.string());

steps.label: Create a compound multivalue index — scalar entityType + array tags
Find all holdings tagged 'dividend'
steps.code:
SELECT ae.data.symbol.string() AS symbol,
       ae.data.quantity.number() AS quantity,
       ae.data.marketValue.number() AS market_value,
       json_serialize(ae.data.tags) AS tags
FROM advisory_entities ae
WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.tags?(@ == "dividend")')
  AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding';

steps.label: Intersect two multivalue indexes — find S&P 500 holdings in technology
steps.code:
SELECT ae.data.symbol.string() AS symbol,
       ae.data.marketValue.number() AS market_value
FROM advisory_entities ae
WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.tags?(@ == "sp500")')
  AND JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.sectors?(@ == "technology")')
  AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding';

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM user_indexes WHERE index_name IN ('IDX_TAGS', 'IDX_SECTORS')
exercises.checkpoint: You just created multi-value indexes on JSON arrays, including a compound index combining a scalar field with an array. The optimizer intersects separate multivalue indexes via bitmap conversion — reliably combining multiple index results where other query planners often fall back to a single index.

exercises.title: SQL Analytics on Collection Data
exercises.description: Cross-entity aggregation, tag analytics, and sector exposure reports — queries that pure document databases cannot optimize.
exercises.explanation:
This is where the convergence advantage becomes undeniable. On a pure document database, cross-entity analytics require a multi-stage aggregation pipeline — `$unwind` to flatten arrays, `$group` to aggregate, `$sort` to order — with no cost-based optimization. The pipeline executes procedurally, stage by stage, in the order you write it. On Oracle, you express the same logic as declarative SQL and the optimizer chooses the execution plan.

[svg:m3-json-table-shred.svg]

**JSON_TABLE** is the bridge between document and relational worlds. It shreds JSON arrays into relational rows using a COLUMNS clause that maps JSON paths to typed SQL columns. The syntax `JSON_TABLE(ae.data, '$.tags[*]' COLUMNS (tag VARCHAR2(50) PATH '$'))` takes each element of the tags array and produces one row per element, with the value projected as a typed `VARCHAR2` column. Those rows are first-class relational data — they participate in GROUP BY, SUM, COUNT, window functions, CTEs, and joins exactly like any table column. The tag analytics query explodes each holding's tags array into individual rows, then groups and aggregates across the entire book of business.

The sector exposure report goes further: it uses a **CTE (Common Table Expression)** with `SUM() OVER()` as a window function to calculate each sector's percentage of total portfolio value in a single pass. No temp tables, no materialized subqueries, no application-side math. The window function `ROUND(sector_value / SUM(sector_value) OVER () * 100, 1)` computes the denominator once across the entire result set and divides each row's value by it. Try writing that in a MongoDB aggregation pipeline — you need `$facet` to split into parallel pipelines, one for the total and one for the per-sector values, then `$merge` to reassemble them.

This is exactly why single-collection design is more powerful on Oracle than on any pure document store. You model as documents for developer velocity and storage efficiency — small, focused entities with natural attributes and embedded arrays. Then you query as relational for analytical power — GROUP BY, window functions, CTEs, joins across entity types. The optimizer figures out the fastest execution path based on real-time statistics. You get the document developer experience for writes and the SQL analytical engine for reads.

**Try this in the editor:**
- `SELECT jt.tag, ae.data.accountId.number() AS account, COUNT(*) FROM advisory_entities ae, JSON_TABLE(ae.data, '$.tags[*]' COLUMNS (tag VARCHAR2(50) PATH '$')) jt WHERE ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding' GROUP BY jt.tag, ae.data.accountId.number() ORDER BY 1, 2` — see tag distribution per account
exercises.code:
-- TAG ANALYTICS: Holdings count by tag (across entire book)
SELECT jt.tag,
       COUNT(*) AS holding_count,
       SUM(ae.data.marketValue.number()) AS total_value
FROM advisory_entities ae,
JSON_TABLE(ae.data, '$.tags[*]' COLUMNS (tag VARCHAR2(50) PATH '$')) jt
WHERE ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding'
GROUP BY jt.tag
ORDER BY total_value DESC;

-- SECTOR EXPOSURE REPORT: Market value by sector with percentage
WITH sector_values AS (
    SELECT jt.sector,
           SUM(ae.data.marketValue.number()) AS sector_value
    FROM advisory_entities ae,
    JSON_TABLE(ae.data, '$.sectors[*]' COLUMNS (sector VARCHAR2(50) PATH '$')) jt
    WHERE ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding'
    GROUP BY jt.sector
)
SELECT sector,
       sector_value,
       ROUND(sector_value / SUM(sector_value) OVER () * 100, 1) AS pct_of_portfolio
FROM sector_values
ORDER BY sector_value DESC;

steps.label: Tag analytics — holdings count and total value by tag
steps.code:
SELECT jt.tag,
       COUNT(*) AS holding_count,
       SUM(ae.data.marketValue.number()) AS total_value
FROM advisory_entities ae,
JSON_TABLE(ae.data, '$.tags[*]' COLUMNS (tag VARCHAR2(50) PATH '$')) jt
WHERE ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding'
GROUP BY jt.tag
ORDER BY total_value DESC;

steps.label: Sector exposure report — market value by sector with portfolio percentage
steps.code:
WITH sector_values AS (
    SELECT jt.sector,
           SUM(ae.data.marketValue.number()) AS sector_value
    FROM advisory_entities ae,
    JSON_TABLE(ae.data, '$.sectors[*]' COLUMNS (sector VARCHAR2(50) PATH '$')) jt
    WHERE ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding'
    GROUP BY jt.sector
)
SELECT sector,
       sector_value,
       ROUND(sector_value / SUM(sector_value) OVER () * 100, 1) AS pct_of_portfolio
FROM sector_values
ORDER BY sector_value DESC;

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM (SELECT jt.tag FROM advisory_entities ae, JSON_TABLE(ae.data, '$.tags[*]' COLUMNS (tag VARCHAR2(50) PATH '$')) jt WHERE ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding' GROUP BY jt.tag)
exercises.checkpoint: You just ran SQL aggregations across entity types using JSON_TABLE and window functions — the analytical power of SQL applied directly to document collection data.

exercises.title: Multi-Value Index + Duality View — Convergence
exercises.description: Combine single-collection design with duality views — multi-value index identifies matches, SQL JOIN navigates relationships, duality view shapes the output.
exercises.explanation:
This is the convergence payoff — **three paradigms in one query**, one optimizer, one round trip. The multi-value index on the entity collection identifies holdings tagged 'dividend' via B-tree lookup, avoiding any collection scan. The SQL JOIN navigates from the collection entity to the relational accounts table using the natural **accountId** attribute — no string parsing, no key decoding. The duality view projects the final output as a rich JSON document with nested accounts and holdings — the shape your API consumer expects.

[svg:m3-convergence-query.svg]

Trace the execution path. The optimizer starts with the multi-value index on tags — a B-tree probe for 'dividend' that returns only matching document rowids. For each matching holding, it extracts `accountId` via dot-notation and probes the accounts table's primary key index — a nested loop join, the fastest possible join method for small result sets. From each account row, it extracts `client_id` and joins to the duality view, which projects the full client portfolio as a nested JSON document with accounts and holdings arrays assembled from the underlying relational tables. Three data sources — a JSON collection, a relational table, and a duality view — unified in one execution plan.

On a polyglot architecture, this query would require three separate systems: a document store for the entity collection, a relational database for client and account data, and an aggregation layer to stitch them together. Each boundary crossing adds network latency, consistency risk, and operational overhead. Three round trips, three failure modes, three systems to monitor, patch, and scale. Here it is one SQL statement, and the cost-based optimizer decides whether to use nested loop joins, hash joins, or merge joins based on real-time statistics.

This pattern is the architectural thesis of the entire workshop: **model the domain, project the access, let the optimizer handle the physics**. You stored entities as documents for write efficiency and developer ergonomics. You created indexes on natural attributes for fast retrieval. You used multi-value indexes for array-level querying. You joined across paradigms — collection to relational to duality view — in a single atomic operation. No ETL. No sync. No application-side stitching. That is convergence.

**Try this in the editor:**
- `SELECT json_serialize(cp.data PRETTY) AS client_portfolio FROM advisory_entities ae JOIN accounts a ON a.account_id = ae.data.accountId.number() JOIN client_portfolio_dv cp ON json_value(cp.data, '$._id' RETURNING NUMBER) = a.client_id WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.tags?(@ == "growth")') AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding'` — change the tag filter to 'growth' to see different client portfolios
exercises.code:
-- Find clients whose holdings include "dividend" tagged securities
-- using the multi-value index on the entity collection,
-- then join to the relational model for the full client document
SELECT json_serialize(cp.data PRETTY) AS client_portfolio
FROM advisory_entities ae
JOIN accounts a ON a.account_id = ae.data.accountId.number()
JOIN client_portfolio_dv cp ON json_value(cp.data, '$._id' RETURNING NUMBER) = a.client_id
WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.tags?(@ == "dividend")')
  AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding';
exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM advisory_entities ae WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.tags?(@ == "dividend")') AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding'
exercises.checkpoint: You just combined multi-value index lookup, SQL JOIN on a natural attribute, and duality view projection in a single query. Three paradigms. One optimizer. One round trip.
